title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series: Ep08 - Flux 1: Schnell and Dev Installation Guide
description:
Welcome to Episode 8 of our ComfyUI tutorial series! In this episode, we're exploring FLUX, a new model from Black Forest Labs. I'll walk you through installing and using the different versions, like Dev and Schnell, while comparing their performance and highlighting the best settings. Discover which model works best for your needs and how to integrate FLUX into your workflow.

We’ll also share some tips and tricks to help you get the most out of FLUX, ensuring smooth and efficient results. Check out the description for all the links and resources you'll need to follow along!

Download all the workflows from the pixaroma-workflows channel on Discord. The links are also organized there.
https://discord.gg/gggpkVgBf3

Flux Examples 
https://comfyanonymous.github.io/ComfyUI_examples/flux/

----

Download Flux Schnell fp8 from here (Faster)
https://huggingface.co/Comfy-Org/flux1-schnell/tree/main
Place it in the folder: ..ComfyUI/models/checkpoints/

----

Download Flux Dev fp8 from here (
transcript: Welcome to episode 8 of our comfy UI tutorial series. Today I want to talk about Flux,  the new model that is currently my favorite. I'll show you how to install and use different  versions of it, such as the Dev and Schnell versions. I'll compare the different models,  highlighting which one is better, faster, and which settings work best among other details.  Flux is created by Black Forest Labs, a team of researchers and engineers who have developed  various models in the past, and previously worked for Stability AI. They have now introduced  the Flux version 1 model family, which performs quite well, but requires a good computer for  decent speed. It supports different ratios and resolutions up to two megapixels. They offer  three versions. The Pro version, which isn't available for download, and the Dev and Schnell  versions, which we can download and use on our computers. The Pro version is only available  through the API. The Dev version produced the best quality but requires more resources,  while the Schnell version is the fastest but has lower quality compared to Dev.  The Dev license is non-commercial, while Schnell uses the Apache 2.0 license.  I checked the Dev license on their hugging face page, which includes a license.md file.  It states, we claim no ownership rights in and to the outputs. You are solely responsible for  the outputs you generate and their subsequent uses in accordance with this license. You may  use output for any purpose, including commercial purposes. However, it also notes, you may not use  the output to train fine-tune or distill a model that is competitive with Flux. As I understand it,  you cannot use the Flux model itself for commercial purposes, but you can use the images generated  with it commercially. You also cannot use those images to train a new model that competes with Flux.  For the Schnell model, you can use both the model and its output for commercial work. Again,  this is my interpretation, so you should review the license yourself for complete clarity.  I also asked ChatGPT to look online and explain the license for each version, so you can pause and  read this information if you want. I will put all the links in the description. Here are some  examples from a GitHub page. We have several versions, the regular version, which is Flux Dev,  the largest version of Dev, Flux Schnell, the smallest version, and the simpler to use versions,  which are floating point eight versions that are smaller and don't require complicated workflows.  Both Dev and Schnell have these smaller versions. To make it easier for you, I will show you how  to quickly install each one and you can decide which is better for you by the end of the video.  For the FP8 version, download the models and place them in the checkpoints folder. You can  download them from the links provided, but I will also include additional links. From this page,  you can download the FP8 version of the Dev model, which is 17 gigabytes, download it using the  button provided. Next, navigate to comfy UI, then models, and place the files in the checkpoints folder.  I will also download the Schnell floating point version and place it in the same folder.  It has the same size, but is the Schnell version. Remember, Schnell means fast in German.  After the downloads finish, which might take a few minutes depending on your internet speed,  I will first show you some quick workflows for those who are impatient, and then I will explain  how to build that workflow from a simple SDXL workflow. Save these images somewhere on your drive,  they also include the workflow. I will do the same for the Schnell image.  Next, open comfy UI, click on manager, and then select update all to update comfy UI,  so it adds the new nodes that recognize flux models. After restarting, you should have the latest  version, click load, and navigate to where you downloaded the images for both Schnell and Dev.  Look for the checkpoint in the name to identify the floating point eight version. I will load  the Schnell version, which is faster. It should show you the Schnell model, if not, select it from  the list. Running the workflow should give you an image like this. Now let's load the Dev  version workflow and run it to see what we get. The result is this anime image, so that was the  short version. I'll show you later in the video how to install the larger 16-bit Dev version as well.  To recap the short version, download the models, place them in the checkpoints folder,  download the images, load them in comfy UI, and run the workflow. It's pretty simple. Let's explain  it a bit more. Now, let's load a basic SDXL. You should have one working by now, but you can also  download it from my Discord server. I will show you how the workflow differs compared to SDXL.  First, we need to load the flux model. I will start with the Schnell floating point eight version.  Another difference is the empty latent image. It works better if we choose an empty SD3 latent  image, which we used in another episode for stable diffusion three medium. Delete the old one and  reconnect the new one to the latent input. I will change the color to purple so it's easier to see.  I'm also changing the color of the positive prompt to green and the negative prompt to red,  as these are the colors most people commonly use. I'm deleting the negative prompt since flux  doesn't support it. Then, I'll right click and choose collapse to save space. As you might recall,  each model has recommended settings. On the examples page, you'll find the settings for the K  sampler. The CFG needs to be set to one because when it is one, it ignores the negative prompt.  For Schnell, being the fast version, it only needs four steps to run. The sampler should be set  to Euler and the scheduler should be set to simple. Let's give it a test to see if it works and  here's the result. Not bad for only four steps. The first time you try it, it will take longer  because the model needs to load, which took about 13 seconds. However, after that, it should be  quicker. On my RTX 4090, it only takes three four seconds, which is great. I'll save the workflows  from this video so you can load them quickly and you can easily download them from my discord.  Now, let's load the dev floating point eight version. This one has the same settings except that  the number of steps should be higher around 20, 30 steps. I'll go with 20, then I'll make some  space for a new node called flux guidance. The default value is 3.5 and since our CFG is locked to one,  we can use this guidance node to influence the image. It's somewhat similar to CFG, but not quite  the same. This node doesn't affect the shell version, it only works with the dev version.  Connect the conditioning from the positive prompt and then connect the conditioning to the  positive input on the K sampler. If I run the workflow, you'll see that the first time it took 24  seconds because we have more steps now. However, the second time I ran it, it only took 14 seconds.  On an older PC with six gigabytes of VRAM, it was still runnable but took around six minutes,  so having a newer powerful Nvidia card will definitely help with the speed. I'm also saving  this workflow so you can download it. Now, let's test the flux guidance a little. I'll use a prompt  for a painting of a gnome and choose a fixed seed so we can compare the results. Running it with  the default value gives us this result. If I change the guidance to three, you can see the difference.  You can go lower, but at some point it will start to degrade the image. It might create a nice  painting effect, but it's best to experiment to find what looks best for your prompt. The result  varies depending on the prompt, but I usually lower the value for paintings. Sometimes higher  values can also help, and you can use values between 1 and 100, though values above 20 have less  effect. Higher values might improve text in some cases, but the default value is usually a good  starting point. I'm loading the Schnell version workflow again since it's faster. Someone asked if  flux can use styles, and yes, it can. But first, make sure to check episode seven to get the extra  nodes and set up the styles correctly. I'm adding the multiple style selector node. Next,  I'll convert the clip text encoder to text input and make some space for another node. I'll add an  easy positive and then a text concatenate. Just like in episode seven, I'll connect positive to  text A and positive string to text B. Then I'll connect the string to text input. This way  are prompt along with the prompt from the styles is combined in this node before going to the clip  text encoder. I'll collapse and rearrange the nodes to make it clearer. Now I can add a prompt and  select one of the styles such as cartoon cute. When I run the workflow, it seems to work just fine.  I'm changing the seed to fixed so I can compare the style influence. First, I'll add a prompt like  a gnome in a magical forest. Then I'll deactivate the styles by selecting no style. Running the workflow  gives us this image. If I switch to a new style with the same seed and prompt, such as the impressionism  painting style, the result should be different and we get something like this.  From my tests, flux recognizes fewer art styles compared to SDX cell, but it still influences the  image since it uses additional prompts. It might not capture the exact style if flux doesn't recognize  it. Let's try another style such as portrait cinematic from photography. The result is a more  photographic look of the gnome. Finally, let's test a different style like biomechanical art to see  how it influences the result. We get a sci-fi gnome, which is pretty interesting. I will create a more  compact version to make it easier for beginners to use. You'll be able to just download and use it.  First, select all the nodes except the save image node, then right-click and choose convert to group  node, give it a name, and now we have a simplified interface. I'll hide the negative prompt from the  interface since it's not used in flux. You can try adding phrases like no signature or no watermark  to the positive prompt. Sometimes these can work. You can add from one to four styles here with the  first one being the most important so the order does matter. From the width and height settings,  you can adjust the size. Since we use the SD3 empty latent image, it handles multiples of 16,  making it more compatible. I'll make some more changes in adjustments, and you'll be able to  download a version that looks something like this. I want to test both the schnell and dev floating point  eight versions, so I have the schnell workflow on top and the dev workflow on the bottom. I want both  workflows to use the same prompt, so I'll convert the positive prompt in both to text input instead.  You can add a primitive node and connect it to both workflows with the text encoder.  Alternatively, if you have the nodes from episode seven, the comfy easy use node already  includes the positive prompt in green and ready to use. I'll add a prompt here and start with a fix  prompt for both workflows. I might change it to increment, so I can test with another seed while  keeping the same seed for both workflows. I'll create a more compact version, leaving only the  generations and the prompt visible on the screen. The first one will be the schnell version and the  second will be the dev version. If I test the prompt, green sphere on a blue cube with a black  background in 3D render style, both versions seem to understand it. However, for some of the seeds,  I ended up with a blurred image as seen in this case. I'll show you how to fix this by changing  to a different sampler and scheduler toward the end of the video. I recorded this video over a few  days because each day new information, versions, and improvements emerged online. I've tried to  include as many updates as possible in this video. For a portrait of a woman, I got nice results.  The dev version seems to be a bit more realistic, but if you want even more realism, there is now a  realistic Laura available for flux. For a portrait of a Viking in the rain at golden hour, the dev version  produced a more realistic look and successfully captured the rain, which the schnell version did not.  For a witch holding a sign with text, the dev version nearly got it right on the first try.  On the second try, both models produced the correct result. While it's not perfect,  it's the best offline model I've tried so far. For a cartoon warrior cat night, the dev version  handles the swords and shield better. I also noticed that the dev version generally has fewer  mistakes with hands and details compared to the schnell version. For a penguin eating ice cream  with a polar bear in the background, the dev version did a better job once again. The schnell version  failed to render the ice cream. For the prompt with a fox in a bunny, the dev version has more  details in the image. I then wanted a portrait of a crocodile with a wanted poster on the wall. I  decided to add $1,000 reward to the poster and wanted the crocodile to actually be featured on it.  If you're clear with your prompt, you can get quite good results. Once again, the dev version  seems to be a bit better at this. I also tried a simple prompt like Stairs to Heaven in concept art  and the styles appeared a bit different. Then I got hungry and tested a photo of a hamburger  which resulted in a quite delicious image. Next I wanted a small flag that says free and wondered  what if the flag was white like a surrender flag. The model was able to incorporate all these  elements demonstrating quite good prompt understanding. I wanted to do some vector style design as well  and again, the dev version tended to blur the image for certain seeds. Check later in the video to see  how I fix that issue. However, you can create some nice vector designs with flux. For the dev version,  I got a charming cute cat. It's also quite good for logos from simple to more complex designs.  You can experiment with styles and gradients to come up with some great logo ideas.  You can also create some great photos for your presentations like cool mockups. As you can see,  the dev version still tends to produce better results. If your video card can handle it and  you're willing to wait a bit longer for generations, it's definitely worth it. For the oil painting  of a king on a throne, both versions produce some nice results. But I still think the dev version  is a bit better. Who would have imagined that you could get a digital painting of something so  complex in just a few seconds on your computer? It's also quite good with hands. So far, the hands  are better than on any other model I've tested. So remember, I mentioned I'd show you a fix  for blurred images. The issue is with the dev model. If I use a specific seed, I get a sharp,  clear image. However, testing subsequent seeds often results in blurred images, which can be  quite frustrating. What fixed it for me was changing the sampler to DPM plus plus 2M and the  scheduler to SGM uniform. Testing the same seed now yields a sharp image. I also tested the  previous seeds and they worked just fine. I'll probably update the workflows on my discord,  include these settings so you don't end up with blurred images. So we've tested the  floating point versions of both dev and schnell. Now, for those with top of the line,  video cards, and plenty of system RAM, who want to try what is considered the best version,  here's how to set it up. The regular version works a bit differently. You'll need some additional  clip models to place into the clip folder. First, go to this link and download these two models.  Navigate to your models folder, then the clip folder, and save the files there.  Next, get the second model, which is the larger one. If you don't have enough RAM,  you can use the version with floating point eight inches in the name and place it in the same  folder. However, the floating point 16 version is recommended if you have more than 32GB of system  RAM. You'll also need the VAE file, which you can find here under the name AE Safe Tensor.  Download it and place it in your models folder, specifically in the VAE folder.  For the actual flux model, this time you need to place it in the unit folder.  Download this large model, which is 23GB, and we'll take some time to download,  save it in the models folder, then into the unit folder. Finally, you need one more thing,  save the image that includes the workflow, load that workflow, and how it looks.  So, this workflow is a bit different from what we're used to. I'll give it a test.  You can find more information about where to place the models on this note.  The result is this animate image. In the tips, you also have the option to change the D type,  which might speed things up depending on your setup. For me, the default option was faster,  but you can change it from here. This setup includes a node that loads both clip models,  we downloaded, and the VAE is separate compared to the floating point eight version we started with,  where the model included all the clips and VAE making it simpler to use.  This version is a bit more complex. We still have the flux guidance, but there's an additional  basic guide, and a custom advanced sampler instead of the case sampler. It also uses an empty  latent image with width and height adjustments on the side. This setup seems more complex,  with automatic adjustments based on resolution, which can be confusing. I always try to simplify  workflows, but others seem to make them more complex. It would be nice to have a single node  that handles all these steps. The first time I ran it, it took over 35 seconds to generate,  but the second generation was completed in just 18 seconds.  Now let's test both the regular dev version, the large one, and compare it with the  floating point eight version, which is a bit smaller. I'll convert the positive prompt text  encoder to a text input so I can use an easy positive node, allowing me to place the prompt in  one location, the seed will be fixed. I'll arrange the setup so you can see both generations  and the prompt. I'll place the regular version on the left and the smaller floating point eight  version on the right. For the first try, we see less detail in the eye with the floating point eight  version. For the gnome, the results are quite similar, but the regular version has more detail,  like the moon, having more details. For the pixel Roma book, both versions got the text right on  the first try. The same goes for the sign on an exotic island with only some small differences.  For the burger photo, the results are again very similar. Most of the time, it doesn't really seem  worth using the regular version since the results are comparable. It depends on the prompt.  Sometimes you see a difference and sometimes you don't. The floating point eight version can do  nice illustrations and decent photos and with the new realistic Laura, you can achieve even more  realism. However, some people have noticed a small line on the chin in photos. It handles a girl in  the grass better than stable diffusion three and can even manage a yoga pose, though I used a  witch as the subject. Overall, the regular version is a bit better but not by much. Given the extra  complexity in the workflow and the increased generation time, I'll probably stick with the floating  point eight version most of the time. I was about to finish this video when new updates came out.  For those with lower VRAM, there are now faster versions of flux. The quality is a bit lower,  but at least you can check if it runs on your PC. The new version is NF4, which I believe  stands for normalized float for a bit. I'll show you how I managed to install both the dev and  shell versions on my comfy UI. Download the shell version model from the link in the description  or on Discord where it's more organized. Next, in your model's folder, look for the checkpoints  folder. This version worked for me when it had B&B in the name. I tried a version without B&B  in the name and it didn't work, so make sure the name includes B&B. Do the same for the dev model.  These models are smaller than the floating point eight versions and only take up 11 gigabytes.  After downloading, you should have both models ready. To make it work on comfy UI, you'll need a  custom node called bits and bytes. As of making this video, it wasn't available in the manager  only in the dev channel, so I'll install it manually. You'll also need to install bits and bytes.  Go to your comfy UI folder and look for the custom nodes folder. Here, you can see all your installed  custom nodes. If you ever install a node and comfy UI stops working, you can delete the folder for  that node from here. Next, go to the address bar in the custom nodes folder, type cmd and press  enter. This will open the command window in that exact path. Use the command get clone followed  by the link to the nodes get hub page. Press enter and the node will be copied to your PC. Now, you  need to install bits and bytes. In the command window, use the command pip install, you bits and  bytes and press enter. It shows here that I already have it installed. If it doesn't install for you  on the portable comfy UI, try this command. Go to your update folder, type cmd and use the same command.  Make sure to close all command windows you have opened or comfy UI will not start. Then run comfy UI.  To ensure everything is working correctly, update comfy UI using the manager and select update all,  then restart comfy UI. Here's the workflow for the schnell floating point 8 version.  To make it work with NF4, you'll need to make a change. Search for nnef in the nodes,  and since we've installed the new custom node, you should see the checkpoint loader NF4 in the list.  Add this node, which looks similar to the load checkpoint node. Reconnect the nodes as you did with  load checkpoint. Connect the model to model, clip to both positive and negative encoder,  and VAE to VAE decode. After making these connections, you can delete the load checkpoint node,  ensure you select the flux schnell NF4 version from the list. When you test it, you might notice that  the first time it's slower, but after that, it should take around three seconds on my RTX 4090.  You can now save the workflow. Next, let's set up the version for the dev NF4.  Select that model from the list, change the steps to 20 to avoid any blurred images with the dev  version, switch to dpm plus plus 2m with sgm uniform. After making these changes, do a quick test to  ensure everything works correctly. For the dev version, you can add the flux guidance node,  which provides extra control. Connect this node between the positive prompt and the key sampler.  Now, let's test the floating point 8 model alongside the NF4 model to compare the results.  First, choose a fixed seed for both models to ensure a better comparison. Next, convert the positive  prompt to text input, so you can use a single prompt for both workflows. Arrange the images so  you can see both side by side on the screen along with the prompt. Place the floating point 8  version on the left and the NF4 version on the right. For some prompts, there's a noticeable  difference between the images, while for others, the difference isn't as significant. Generally,  the floating point 8 version seems to have more detail. For instance, in the robot image,  it appears more detailed around the eyes, and the texture is a bit sharper. For some prompts,  like the burger, the textures are almost the same, but the floating point 8 version shows slightly  more texture. With the cute hamster, the differences in sharpness and detail are more evident  with the NF4 version being less sharp. In the dragon prompt, the images are similar, but the horns  look different. For the pirate face, it's noticeably sharper in the floating point 8 version.  The monkey image is again quite similar, with a bit more texture in the wood in the floating point  8 version. For the magician bunny, the bunny is sharper in the floating point 8 version.  So, depending on your preferences, you might find the floating point 8 version more appealing.  For the portrait, it has more texture on the nose and forehead. For the last part, since I have  already extended the video a few times to include the latest updates, I want to discuss using Laura  with flux. You need a Laura that matches the base model, so in this case, use the dev version,  I'll use the floating point 8 version for this example. X Labs has released a realism Laura  for flux. I found a version that works with comfy UI on this page. It's quite small, so you can  download it and place it in the following folder. Go to comfy UI, then models, and look for the  Laura's folder. Save the Laura there. I found a prompt that better demonstrates what this Laura can do.  First, let's hit refresh so we can see the new downloaded model when we add it to the workflow.  But before adding the Laura, let's test without it using a fixed seed.  The result is this image, which has a plastic look and isn't very realistic.  I'll copy this image and paste it next to the new results for comparison. To add the Laura,  do it as usual. Add a load Laura node and select the Laura from the list.  Place the Laura between the low checkpoint and the clip text encoders. Instead of a direct  connection, route the connection through the Laura. Connect model to model input, clip to clip,  then clip to positive and clip to negative. Now, let's test it to see any differences.  With the realism Laura, the result is much more realistic. If you want to generate more  realistic images, this Laura can help. I'll try another prompt like a portrait of a dog.  First, I'll bypass the Laura to see how it looks without it. The result is quite realistic. I'll  make a copy and paste it. For some reason, it kept loading different images instead of pasting  the correct one. I had to click on the arrows to find my pasted version. It seems I had a folder  in my input directory with more images I had pasted in the past with the same name.  I cleared that folder to fix the issue. I'm going to the load Laura node and right clicking to  bypass again to activate it. When I test it now, the difference is subtle, but noticeable.  You can see that the image on the left with the Laura activated appears more realistic.  In this video, I tested five flux models. As you can see, flux version one comes in different  sizes and versions. Generally, the larger the file, the better the image quality, but it also  requires a more powerful graphics card with more VRAM and more system RAM. Here, you can see the  approximate time it took for each model to generate the images. I tested them on an RTX 4000 and 90.  As a general rule, the dev version offers better quality than the schnell version.  Additionally, the smaller the file size, the faster it will generate. If you don't have a high-end  graphics card, start with the schnell version. If your graphics card is decent, but not the best,  you can try the dev floating point 8 version. On some cards, the NF4 version might be the fastest  option. It took me a while to organize everything, but now you can find all the workflows for episode 8  and earlier episodes on the Discord channel, Pixiroma workflows. I've organized them by model.  For example, the full version includes an easy access download link for everything you need  and instructions on where to place the files. You also have a preview of the workflow,  which you can download and load into comfy UI using the load button. I've also created workflows  for the dev version and floating point 8 complete with settings and other useful information.  I've included compact versions for most workflows, added Laura and more. You can test these for free  on various websites. Sometimes they can be busy. If that's the case, try the second website,  which should load fine. You can add a prompt, run it, and see that it's waiting for a GPU to become  available. Once it is, you'll get an image that you can download. I have a small request, 78% of  people who watch my videos are not subscribed. If you could hit that subscribe button, it would  really help. If you found this video useful, please leave a like and a comment to help my channel  grow so I can create even more content for you. Thank you for watching and have a great day.
